[29:00] that in one pill. Their iron and ferritin
[29:03] metabolism is crucial. We talked about iron
[29:05] and ferritin last week. If you didn't get a
[29:07] chance to see that, the videos are posted in
[29:11] the course in lesson number one, two, week
[29:16] two. We talked about iron and ferritin. So
[29:20] you can go back and watch that video.
[29:23] Proteins are crucial for every single thing.
[29:25] Remember proteins are the building blocks of
[29:27] the cell. You're not going to make cellular
[29:29] functions work without protein. And so that's
[29:33] going to be crucial. And then, of course, that
[29:35] boils down to a healthy gut, digestion, and
[29:37] absorption. They might eat 100 grams of
[29:40] protein a day, but their protein, their
[29:42] globulin level might be 1.6 simply because
[29:45] they're just not absorbing it. So we have to
[29:48] get to the nutrient support and the gut

[34:06] the search field, you should find
[34:08] my stuff. So how do we evaluate a
[34:13] thyroid set of labs? The first
[34:15] thing we want to do is look at
[34:17] unmedicated. Those are the first
[34:19] people coming to you that you're
[34:21] there. It's a blank slate. So
[34:24] when you get these labs back,
[34:27] ideally, you're going to want that
[34:28] TSH to be less than two, I really
[34:32] prefer it to be less than 1.5
[34:34] most of the time. At 1.6, 1.7, you
[34:38] do see your ROS scores, those
[34:41] questionnaire scores go higher and
[34:43] higher. So I really like that TSH
[34:46] to be a little less than the two.
[34:49] We want the free T4 somewhere
[34:51] around one. I don't care if it's
[34:53] 0.7, 0.8, 1.04. I don't really
[34:56] care because in normal pathophysiology
[35:00] free T4 is supposed to convert
[35:01] into free T3. In most of these
[35:03] patients, it's not. For whatever
[35:06] reason, they've lost the ability
[35:09] for conversion to occur properly.
[35:11] And so they will have free T4
[35:13] that's okay, and free T3 that's
[35:16] really low. So our goal
[35:19] unmedicated is to have a free
[35:22] T3 somewhere around 3.4, 3.5.
[35:25] If their free T4 is 1.2, 1.6,
[35:32] high or upper end of the range, and
[35:36] they have a free T3 lower than
[35:38] 3.5, they are not converting
[35:40] well. So we can't normally
[35:45] correct conversions, like that's
[35:47] genetic. Sometimes it's
[35:49] inflammatory. Sometimes it's
[35:51] related to their Hashimoto's.
[35:53] Sometimes it's a whole MTHFR comp
[35:56] T issue like way other rabbits
[36:00] to chase. And I did put a video
[36:04] I recorded a long time ago, two
[36:06] or three years ago, in the
[36:08] lesson in week three's lesson in
[36:11] the course. It's about MTHFR and
[36:14] thyroid problems. And so I
[36:16] covered the science and the
[36:18] relationship. And so that we

[36:24] a whole other animal like it's
[36:25] just complex. But that is over
[36:29] there. And y'all can watch that
[36:31] at your leisure. But free T3
[36:33] unmedicated should be somewhere
[36:35] around 3.5. If it's not, then
[36:39] they may not need medicine. They
[36:41] may just need vitamin D or iron
[36:43] or protein or iodine. That may
[36:46] be the problem. If their TSH is
[36:48] 2.6 and their free T3 is 3.1.

[36:54] leiothirone. They probably only

[37:09] Most of these people in that
[37:11] three to 3.5 ish range mostly
[37:18] need iodine. They probably are
[37:21] not using iodized salt. Most
[37:24] people not using iodized salt

[37:29] to 24 months. So Kayla just
[37:34] shared her lab results in the

[38:01] where lots of things feel
[38:02] better. People don't draw
[38:05] reverse T3 very often, but it
[38:08] is absolutely crucial to your

[38:13] Reverse T3 is what free T4
[38:15] becomes when free T3 is not
[38:18] being converted properly. Okay.
[38:22] Let me say that again. Reverse
[38:24] T3 is what free T4 becomes when
[38:28] it's not converting properly to
[38:30] free T3. Okay. Free T4 to free
[38:35] T3 conversion takes a lot of
[38:37] effort and three major enzymes,
[38:40] diiodinase one, two, and three
[38:43] and other chemicals and pieces to
[38:46] the process. If there is a
[38:48] genetic defect in a person's
[38:50] ability to produce any one of
[38:53] those enzymes or other parts of
[38:57] the process, conversion from free
[38:59] T4 will be impaired and free T3
[39:02] will be sub clinically low. In
[39:04] many cases, you'll see a free T3
[39:06] like 1.5, 1.8, 2.1 and that's
[39:11] overt hypothyroidism right there.
[39:14] But when it's in the three to
[39:16] 3.4, 3.5 range, that's a
[39:18] little bit more nuanced and
[39:21] more challenging to try to
[39:22] figure out does this person need
[39:23] a medicine? Do they just need the
[39:25] support? So sometimes patients
[39:28] will choose medicine because they
[39:30] have felt so bad so long and I'm
[39:32] okay with that. The thing is you
[39:34] just have to monitor closely
[39:36] and reevaluate their med use
[39:38] very routinely. You can't let
[39:41] them go three or four or five,
[39:42] six months not monitoring because
[39:45] they can wind up hyper. Our goal
[39:50] for reverse T3 unmedicated is
[39:52] about 15. If it's 16 or 17, I'm
[39:56] not going to panic or freak out.
[39:58] But if it starts getting to be
[39:59] 18 or 19, that's more of an
[40:01] inflammation driven level, not
[40:04] normal physiological range. Okay.
[40:08] If the free T3 and the reverse
[40:11] T3 are both in iron range,

[40:15] but they're also not hyper and
[40:16] that's whether they're
[40:17] medicated or not. So keep that in

[40:34] comments in the chat. If you
[40:35] want to follow that, nothing
[40:37] worth responding to. In
[40:39] medicated patients, your reverse
[40:41] T3 is going to help you

[40:54] they probably need way less T4

[41:07] they are going to need less T4,

[41:13] Armor or NP, their medicine

[41:48] from either 100 to 88 or 100 to

[41:52] 100 to 91? You don't have a 91.

[42:30] Reverse T3 is very sensitive to
[42:33] inflammatory processes,
[42:35] infections, stress. So watch
[42:40] that over time. Once you get
[42:42] your reverse T3 to go at
[42:44] 10 or 12, and they're coming
[42:47] back maybe six or eight months
[42:48] later, they're, you know,
[42:49] they've, they've been coming for
[42:51] a while and you've gotten them
[42:53] way out. And they haven't been
[42:57] back for a while. And
[42:59] suddenly, their reverse T3 is
[43:02] 26. You're like, what happened?
[43:05] Did you overdose on your T4?
[43:06] No. Ask them about a sickness.
[43:09] Did they just get over the
[43:10] flu? Did they have a strep
[43:12] infection, a UTI, a cellulitis?
[43:15] Did their child wind up in the
[43:18] hospital? Did their grown
[43:20] daughter get married? That's
[43:22] stressful for parents. Like all
[43:24] of those kinds of things are
[43:26] going to play a role in that
[43:29] reverse T3. So don't
[43:31] automatically draw their
[43:33] Levo dose just because there's
[43:34] some random jump or spike in
[43:37] that reverse T3. Talk to your

[43:41] have driven it up. Were you
[43:42] sick around the time of your
[43:43] lab draw? Did you get sick right
[43:46] before or right after your lab
[43:48] draw? Remember, incubation
[43:51] periods for colds, flus and
[43:52] viruses are different. So if
[43:55] I got exposed to a flu last
[43:59] weekend, this is four days
[44:01] later, my immune system in four
[44:03] days is going to be ramped up
[44:05] trying to get ready to fight
[44:06] off this illness. I don't have
[44:07] any symptoms yet, but I'm sick
[44:10] with the flu. And so my labs
[44:13] might show that. So keep that
[44:15] in the back of your mind that
[44:17] reverse T3 is driven by
[44:19] inflammation, illness and
[44:21] stress. So keep an eye on
[44:25] that surgery, another stress,
[44:28] like hip surgery, I fell and
[44:30] brought my arm, I had to have

[44:38] can run that reverse T3 up. So

[44:58] want. So free T3, this is the

[45:05] mitochondria for energy

[45:12] cell, but it never enters the

[48:34] treat it mostly like it's a T4
[48:36] product. So that means this

[48:39] stomach, an hour at minimum
[48:42] away from everything else except
[48:44] lio. You can add doses of lio
[48:46] to armor or NP, but you
[48:49] cannot take armor or NP or
[48:51] Levo with any other medicine.
[48:55] No PPI, no metformin, no

[49:01] a massive affinity to lots of
[49:04] other molecules and chemistries.

[49:09] their T4 product in combination

[49:13] their stomach alone and no

[49:17] no drinks, no protein, no
[49:19] nothing for at least a full
[49:21] hour after they've taken that.
[49:24] I typically call levothyroxine
[49:27] or T4, levo, four letters.

[49:43] them straightened out.

[49:49] becoming less and less

[49:55] issues as well. I think as

[50:11] out there in the world, the

[50:22] to use all sorts of things to
[50:24] help their thyroid medicine
[50:26] work better. The easiest thing
[50:28] is just add lio. You give them
[50:30] the exact molecule they're
[50:31] missing and it fixes tons of
[50:33] problems. So if I have a patient
[50:40] on levo with a reverse T3 of 15

[50:46] they're on levo 100, I'm

[50:53] reverse T3 will fall. That's
[50:57] not a big problem. If their
[50:58] reverse T3 is staying pretty
[51:00] good in our okay range 9 to 15,

[51:12] around 3.4 or 3.5, they're

[51:17] happening. So those might be,
[51:20] I don't think I have anybody

[51:26] end up on Lio. Using T3 for a

[51:34] scary at first because we've
[51:36] been told it's, you know,
[51:37] terrible. It's not a good
[51:39] drug. It's a great drug. The
[51:41] problem it's not used
[51:44] mainstream is it comes with
[51:45] baggage. It's a cardiac
[51:47] stimulant. And so then you
[51:49] have to take time to teach,
[51:51] explain, titrate slowly, have
[51:54] them back frequently. And so
[51:56] that's baggage. That's what we
[51:57] call baggage. It comes with
[52:00] caveats and nuances. And it's
[52:02] just a little bit of a
[52:03] challenge. We in the functional
[52:05] medicine space can't afford to
[52:06] do that because most of us are
[52:08] charging cash. And or we're
[52:11] doing a hybrid practice where
[52:12] we might be charging sometimes
[52:14] insurance, sometimes cash, we
[52:16] might have a membership plan.
[52:18] Like there's lots of ways to
[52:19] make this work for your
[52:21] patients. And so look at the
[52:25] free T3 level, figure out if
[52:27] this patient is going to
[52:29] benefit from some Lio and
[52:32] then start prescribing it. If
[52:37] we start prescribing Lio by
[52:39] itself, because the reverse
[52:41] T3 is 20, why would you want
[52:44] to give that person T4? Why
[52:46] does that person need a Levo
[52:48] product? They don't. If the
[52:50] reverse T3 is 20, they do not
[52:52] need a Levo product. And so you
[52:55] just start them on their Lio,
[52:57] start them on their Lio, have
[52:58] them come back in, you know,
[52:59] eight to 10 weeks, we'll
[53:00] recheck their labs, see how
[53:03] the labs look then. Let's say
[53:05] we've done that two or three
[53:06] times now and the reverse T3
[53:08] is now nine. Okay, it might
[53:11] be time to consider some
[53:12] Levo. So I probably put that
[53:15] patient on Levo 25, half a
[53:17] tab every morning. Okay,
[53:20] because we don't want a
[53:23] reverse T3 to not be present
[53:26] at all. Reverse T3 is their
[53:29] braking system. That's what
[53:30] gives them nighttime. It's
[53:32] what gives them tiredness.
[53:33] It's what helps things in the
[53:35] afternoon settle down so they
[53:37] can go to bed and sleep. And
[53:39] so reverse T3 helps drive
[53:41] part of the circadian rhythm.
[53:43] And so it's important that
[53:44] they have some reverse T3,
[53:48] 9, 10, 11, 12, but not 20 or
[53:51] 30. Like it's not okay to
[53:54] have a reverse T3 that high
[53:55] because then they have brakes
[53:58] and gas at the same time.
[54:00] What happens if you drive your
[54:01] car with the gas and the
[54:03] brakes matched at the same
[54:04] time? That's not a very
[54:06] good turnout. If you've ever
[54:07] watched the NASCAR race at
[54:09] the end where the winner is
[54:10] spinning their tires, that's
[54:12] exactly what they're doing.
[54:13] They have the gas pedal and
[54:15] the brake pedal on the floor.
[54:18] And so they're not doing,
[54:19] they're not going anywhere.
[54:20] They're literally just burning
[54:22] rubber. That's all they're
[54:23] doing. And so that's the way
[54:25] bodies feel when there's too
[54:30] much reverse T3 or too little
[54:34] reverse T3 because it's
[54:36] either braking or gassing.
[54:38] And so we have to be able to
[54:40] understand free T3 is the gas
[54:42] pedal. Reverse T3 is the
[54:44] brake pedal. So if they start,
[54:46] if you start a patient with
[54:48] LIO by itself, probably in six
[54:50] or eight months a year, the
[54:52] reverse T3 will be low enough
[54:54] and you'll be like, oh,

[54:56] now. And you should. These
[55:00] are some of the things that
[55:02] we want to consider when we
[55:03] start to prescribe your LIO
[55:05] Thirony. You want to make
[55:07] sure the ferritin level is in
[55:08] a healthy place. What happens
[55:10] if ferritin is low? Low

[55:14] cardiac symptoms,
[55:16] palpitations, heart racing,
[55:17] tachycardia, dysrhythmia is of
[55:19] all types. Afib is one of the
[55:22] most common. So like, that's
[55:25] everybody out there. So we

[55:44] of palpitations and heart

[55:47] is way too low. If ferritin

[55:52] not their thyroid. Put the

[71:46] ferritin is in a safe
[71:47] place to do so. This is
[71:49] more parts of the, um,

[71:55] free, T three is less than

[86:05] shouldn't have gotten LIO
[86:07] with their ferritin this

[86:12] problem. And so I stopped
[86:13] putting people on T3 at

[87:35] morning and two tablets
[87:36] at lunch on the script.
[87:38] So the pharmacy can't
[87:39] try to make it combined
[87:41] into one pill. Um, LIO
[87:43] only comes in fives, 25s
[87:45] and 50s. So it is a big
[87:47] challenge, um, for, uh,
[87:50] patients to try to get any
[87:53] other dose, unless you
[87:54] compound it, you could
[87:55] write for a 10 or a 12
[87:57] milli microgram tablet
[87:58] for sure. Um, so, um,
[88:02] Kidela asked a question
[88:03] earlier about osteoporosis
[88:05] and thyroid meds. So the
[88:07] reason that's an issue,
[88:09] if you do any research,
[88:10] you start reading in the
[88:11] data. So, uh, endocrinologist
[88:15] came up with this, uh,
[88:18] application of problems
[88:24] with thyroid medication. So
[88:27] what they did is they
[88:28] looked at lots of
[88:30] hyperthyroid patients and
[88:34] they realized that many
[88:36] hyperthyroid patients
[88:37] overstimulated, they have
[88:39] way too much free T3
[88:41] and free T4. Um, those
[88:44] patients were at higher
[88:45] risk for osteoporosis and
[88:48] decided that if you put
[88:51] hypothyroid patients on
[88:54] medicine to give them normal
[88:57] lab ranges, they're also
[89:00] hyperthyroid. And so they
[89:02] can develop osteoporosis.
[89:03] How logical does that sound?
[89:07] That, that's what they
[89:08] did. That's what they did.
[89:11] So yeah, you can't get
[89:13] osteoporosis from thyroid
[89:16] medication when it's
[89:17] appropriately prescribed. Now
[89:20] if you turn loose somebody
[89:21] out there with a year's
[89:22] worth of LIO and they're
[89:24] dosing it willy-nilly, maybe
[89:26] they could develop some
[89:27] osteoporosis, but that is
[89:29] why we cover our tails
[89:31] when we prescribe this. We
[89:33] only give them medicine to
[89:34] get to their next lab
[89:35] ballot, lab test and
[89:37] appointment. I prescribe
[89:39] meds at appointments. I
[89:41] prescribe very few meds
[89:42] outside appointments. That
[89:44] is also your expert advice,
[89:47] your expert skill. Do not
[89:49] do that in between
[89:50] appointments. Now, yeah,
[89:53] sometimes things happen.
[89:54] People go on trips and
[89:55] they're out of the country
[89:56] for a couple of weeks and
[89:58] they run out of meds and
[90:00] they didn't have time to
[90:01] get labs done. And like it
[90:03] happens, but I'm telling
[90:04] you as a general rule, we
[90:06] do not write meds outside
